text: The surgeon carefully separates loops of small bowel adhesions from the pelvic region using laparoscopic techniques, ensuring no inadvertent injuries occur during the process.
The surgeon uses a suction device to assist in the dissection of adherent loops of small bowel, carefully avoiding inadvertent injuries.

The surgeon removes one loop of small bowel adhesion from the pelvis. When excessive oozing obstructs the view, sponges are packed to manage bleeding, and Surgicel may be applied later to assist with hemostasis.

The surgeon works within the retroperitoneal plane, which is visually safer for dissection, to mobilize and free the loops of small bowel densely adherent to the bladder. The dissection progresses on the lateral side to safely detach the bowel.

The surgeon identifies a large hydrosalpinx on the right side and ruptures it, proceeding to drain the fluid. The dissection reveals a deeper cavity, initially concerning for rectum or bladder involvement, but it is determined to be a blind cavity, which is unroofed extensively.

The surgeon performs a rectal leak test and injects methylene blue into the bladder, confirming a blind-ending cavity. The right Annexa is being dissected off the midline, with visible pus emerging from the hole in the rectal stump as the suction device is used.
The surgeon successfully removes the right adnexa, revealing the rectal mesentery in the midline. The surgical field is inspected for any additional pockets or collections deeper down.

The surgeon identifies a hole in the rectal stump and performs a leak test, visibly showing air spurting out from the top. The focus then shifts to the left adnexa, which is revealed to contain a large abscess.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to navigate into the proper anatomical planes, addressing any bleeding by applying sponges and Surgicil to control it effectively.

The surgeon maneuvers around the left tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA), addressing persistent bleeding with Surgicel and sponges to clean the area. The rectal stump lumen is visible, with pus egressing from the left adnexa where it fistulized to the rectal stump leak.
The surgeon compresses the giant left tubal ovarian abscess, causing pus to ooze out visibly, aiming to completely drain the abscess.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to completely free the remaining adnexa, ensuring there are no trapped collections or fistulous communications to the rectum. A larger hole is created in the abscess cavity to ensure complete drainage.

The surgeon has separated both adnexa from the midline structure, the rectum, and is assessing whether to staple or suture the area closed. The uterus is visible in the surgical field. The focus is on addressing the pelvic abscess, with plans for future closure of the end colostomy.
The surgeon assesses the rectal stump, deciding against taking it too low or amputating excessive tissue. The edges are mobilized for a sutured closure using a three OV lock suture technique.
The surgeon takes large bites with a Proline suture to mark the rectum and reinforce the inner absorbable layer, creating a second permanent layer of closure.
The surgeon leaves a long suture tail and cleans the surgical area, performing a leak test on the rectal stump, which shows no leakage. A pelvic drain is visibly placed.
